primer.markdown:
# The `cache_control` annotation is a one-line cost lever

Every request to the Messages API sends your full system prompt and tool schemas over the wire and re-processes them on Anthropic's side. For an agent with a 1,500-token system prompt and four elaborate tool schemas, that is the same ~4 KB of context being tokenised on *every* turn. Prompt caching trades that for a cache read at roughly a tenth of the input-token cost, provided the content is marked with `cache_control`.

The typescript/GUIDE.md caching example on lines 686-709 shows the shape. Instead of passing `system` as a bare string, you pass it as an array of structured blocks: `system: [{ type: "text", text: systemPrompt, cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" } }]`. That `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` annotation tells the server to write a 5-minute cache entry keyed on the exact block content. The first request pays full input cost and populates the cache; every subsequent request whose prefix hashes identically reads from the cache and pays the discounted `cache_read_input_tokens` rate.

Three things to know before you turn this on. First, the cache is **prefix-sensitive**: cached blocks must appear in the same order, earlier in the request, than the non-cached blocks. Second, you observe cache activity via the response's `usage` object — `cache_creation_input_tokens` on the first hit, `cache_read_input_tokens` on each reuse. Those two counters are how you prove the optimisation is actually firing. Third, tool schemas benefit the most: a large schema block marked with `cache_control` saves the same tokens every loop iteration, which matters exactly as much as your agent loops. Module 04's loop is where caching pays off.
primer.key_insight: `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` on a structured system or tools block is a one-line cost cut. The proof lives in `response.usage.cache_read_input_tokens` — if that number is nonzero on turn two, the cache is real.

visual.alt: A two-turn comparison diagram. Turn 1 (labelled 'cache miss'): a large system prompt block flagged with a small gold cache_control badge flows into the API and is processed at full cost; the response carries `usage.cache_creation_input_tokens = 1508`. Turn 2 (labelled 'cache hit'): the same system prompt block (identical bytes) flows into the API, which routes to a cache lookup box instead of the tokenizer; the response carries `usage.cache_read_input_tokens = 1508` and `usage.input_tokens` is only the new user turn. A price tag annotation shows the cache-read rate at roughly 0.1x the uncached rate.
visual.alt_es: Un diagrama de comparacion de dos turnos. Turno 1 (etiquetado 'cache miss'): un bloque grande de system prompt con una pequena insignia dorada cache_control fluye hacia la API y se procesa a costo completo; la respuesta lleva `usage.cache_creation_input_tokens = 1508`. Turno 2 (etiquetado 'cache hit'): el mismo bloque de system prompt (bytes identicos) fluye hacia la API, que enruta a una caja de busqueda en cache en lugar del tokenizador; la respuesta lleva `usage.cache_read_input_tokens = 1508` y `usage.input_tokens` es solo el nuevo turno de usuario. Una etiqueta de precio muestra la tarifa de lectura de cache a aproximadamente 0.1x la tarifa sin cache.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner reads the annotated `sendWithCache` block, identifies the exact position of `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }`, and can explain which `usage` counter proves the cache fired on the second request.

questions.prompt: typescript/GUIDE.md lines 686-709 shows how to pass the system prompt for caching. What structural change must you make relative to the non-cached version?
options: Move the prompt into the `messages` array as the first user turn.
Replace the bare `system: "..."` string with an array of typed blocks: `system: [{ type: "text", text: systemPrompt, cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" } }]`.
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md lines 694-701: the `system` field becomes an array of structured content blocks. Each block may carry a `cache_control` annotation. Only the structured form accepts `cache_control`; the plain-string shortcut from Module 02 does not.

questions.prompt: What value is passed to the `cache_control` field on GUIDE.md line 699?
questions.explanation: GUIDE.md line 699: `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }`. `ephemeral` is the currently supported cache type — a short-lived (~5 minute) cache entry keyed on exact block content. There is no `persistent` variant in the block-level `cache_control` API, and a bare boolean is rejected.

questions.prompt: You wire up `sendWithCache` exactly as GUIDE.md shows and call it twice in a row with the same `systemPrompt`. On the second response, which `usage` field tells you the cache fired?
options: `usage.output_tokens` — it drops by a factor of ten.
`usage.cache_read_input_tokens` — it is nonzero and equals the token count of the cached system block.
`usage.cached: true` — a boolean flag the SDK sets.
questions.explanation: The `usage` object on the response exposes `cache_creation_input_tokens` on the first (miss) request and `cache_read_input_tokens` on subsequent (hit) requests. GUIDE.md calls this behaviour out in the comment on line 708: 'Subsequent requests with same system prompt will use cache.' The counter is how you verify it actually fired — a stubborn zero means something in the prefix differed between calls.
